items.title: Foreign demand for Chinese bonds is surging: Trading volume of Chinese onshore bonds traded by overseas funds via Hong Kong jumped to a record $179 bi...
items.description_html:
Foreign demand for Chinese bonds is surging:

Trading volume of Chinese onshore bonds traded by overseas funds via Hong Kong jumped to a record $179 billion in March.

Chinese onshore bonds comprise government and state-backed Yuan-denominated bonds, which are accessible to foreign investors through the Hong Kong market.

At the same time, average daily turnover surged to an all-time high of $8.1 billion.

Trading volume has more than DOUBLED since October 2025.

This comes as the Iran War has driven global investors to seek alternatives to traditional safe-haven assets such as US Treasuries.

Yuan-denominated bonds have outperformed global peers since the conflict began, supported by China's abundant liquidity and limited exposure to the energy shock.

Foreign investors are piling into Chinese bonds at a record pace.

items.description_html:
It turns out that oil financial and physical markets do converge -- just not in the way that many were expecting.

Dated Brent is now below $100 a barrel (from $145 last week), and physical differentials in the key pricing window have plunged >$10 from a few days ago.

Javier Blas: For Europe, the energy “crisis” in natural gas and electricity is, essentially, over.

Sure, it can re-start if the ceasefire collapses, and Iran were to attack Qatar.

But as now, it’s gone. And, let’s be clear, it was never a threat to start with as 2022 was. Key for ECB/BoE.

items.title: BREAKING: President Trump says Iran has agreed to suspend its nuclear program indefinitely, and will not receive any frozen funds from the US, per Blo...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: President Trump says Iran has agreed to suspend its nuclear program indefinitely, and will not receive any frozen funds from the US, per Bloomberg.

Details include:

1. Trump says that a deal to end the war between the US/Israel and Iran is now "mostly complete"

2. Talks over a lasting agreement will "probably" be held this weekend and "most main points are finalized"

3. Trump denied that the moratorium on Iran’s nuclear program would expire after 20 years

4. Trump has not yet decided who would lead the US delegation for talks to sign the agreement

US oil prices are down to a 5-week low.

items.title: Speculative traders are ramping up US Dollar long positions: Net bullish speculative positioning in the US Dollar is up to +$18 billion, the highest i...
items.description_html:
Speculative traders are ramping up US Dollar long positions:

Net bullish speculative positioning in the US Dollar is up to +$18 billion, the highest in 12 months.

Speculative traders include hedge funds and asset managers that take positions based on price trends and macro views rather than hedging needs.

This marks a sharp reversal from the net -$21 billion short seen in February.

The positioning shift continued despite the US Dollar Index declining over the last 2 weeks.

By comparison, peak bullish positioning reached ~$35 billion in 2019 and early 2025.

Investor demand for the US Dollar is mounting.

items.title: Strait of Hormuz is open during ceasefire, Iran says. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said the Strait of Hormuz was open following a ceasefire...
items.description_html:
Strait of Hormuz is open during ceasefire, Iran says.
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said the Strait of Hormuz was open following a ceasefire accord agreed in Lebanon, while US President Donald Trump said he believed a deal to end the Iran war would come "soon," although the timing remains unclear.
Araghchi said in a post on X the Strait was open for all commercial vessels for the remainder of the U.S.-brokered 10-day truce between Israeli forces and Iran-backed Hezbollah agreed between Israel and Lebanon.
He said the passage of ships would need to be along the route that Iran's Ports and Maritime Organisation had announced.
@Reuters

items.title: JIOFIN delivered strong 96.7% YoY revenue growth driven by 133% interest income and 461% fee income growth, with the loan book expanding 155.7% to ₹2...
items.description_html:
JIOFIN delivered strong 96.7% YoY revenue growth driven by 133% interest income and 461% fee income growth, with the loan book expanding 155.7% to ₹25,710 Cr. However, PAT declined 13.9% as finance costs surged 38x to fund the aggressive lending expansion.

The business model transformation is evident with core operations now comprising 84.8% of revenue. Credit quality remains strong with impairments growing only 14.6% vs 155% loan growth. The new reinsurance JV with Allianz and continued fee income momentum provide near-term catalysts.

Positive Factors

Total income grew 96.7% YoY to ₹1,019.7 Cr

Interest income grew 133% YoY to ₹642.5 Cr

Loan book expanded 155.7% YoY to ₹25,710.8 Cr

Fees and commission surged 461.4% YoY to ₹221.4 Cr

Net Interest Income grew 28.5% YoY to ₹344.4 Cr

Impairments grew only 14.6% YoY vs 155.7% loan growth - strong credit quality

Reinsurance JV with Allianz received IRDAI license

Dividend increased to ₹0.60 per share from ₹0.50

Risk Factors

PAT declined 13.9% YoY to ₹272.2 Cr despite 96.7% income growth

Finance costs surged 38x (3796.6% YoY) to ₹298.1 Cr

NII margin compressed sharply to 53.6% from 97.2% YoY

PPOP declined 12.4% YoY to ₹327.1 Cr

Debt-to-Equity ratio increased to 0.16x from 0.03x

Group CFO resignation effective April 20, 2026

items.description_html:
Strait of Hormuz Reopens

Iran announced the Strait of Hormuz is now “completely open” for commercial traffic.

“In line with the ceasefire in Lebanon, the passage for all commercial vessels through Strait of Hormuz is declared completely open for the remaining period of ceasefire,” Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi wrote in a social media post.

Trump in his latest social media post said that the US naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz will remain in “full force,” until the US’ transaction with Iran is “100% complete.”

items.title: Risk appetite among institutional investors is skyrocketing: Institutional investors purchased ~22% more call than put options last week, the largest ...
items.description_html:
Risk appetite among institutional investors is skyrocketing:

Institutional investors purchased ~22% more call than put options last week, the largest difference in at least 16 months, according to Citadel.

Monday and Tuesday also saw significant institutional call option inflows, now on track for the largest weekly bullish reading since October 2024.

As a result, institutional demand for call options is up to the highest level on record.

Institutional call option purchases over 5 days ending Tuesday have exceeded the average since January 2025 by +37%.

By comparison, during the April 2025 market recovery, the average was surpassed by +25%.

Institutional investors are piling into equities.

items.title: Portfolio should have a mix of all themes in my opinion , few tilted to growth , value and optionality , allocation should be tilted to market dynamic...
items.description_html:
Portfolio should have a mix of all themes in my opinion , few tilted to growth , value and optionality , allocation should be tilted to market dynamics and margin of safety and where the opp size is very large

Getting too aggressive trying to be an extreme value/growth investor , unnecessarily putting yourself into buckets does not help in my opinion , have to find the best of both worlds tilted towards margin of safety and incremental growth and focus on management integrity and quality

People who only tilt to high growth may be stuck in sectors at the end of the cycle , and people who are too value oreiented might be too early to themes that even if stock does well, time based returns are not very impressive
Play as the pitch demands and opportunities present itself not according to biases or buckets

items.description_html:
RT Adam Kobeissi
Directly from Step #10 of our "Conflict Playbook:"

"By the time negotiations become credible, investors are typically defensively allocated. Energy exposure is elevated, equity risk has been reduced, and volatility is elevated due to the implicit uncertainty. When uncertainty suddenly collapses, those positions unwind quickly, as seen in April 2025, August 2025, October 2025, and January 2026."

We can now add April 2026 to this list.

The 10th and final step of our "Conflict Playbook" is on the horizon and the market is surging as expected.

And now, not only has the AI Revolution rapidly accelerated in the background of this 7-week war, but markets are pricing-in an inflation shock.

Those who sold assets in March are rushing back into the market as quality names have only gotten cheaper on a Forward P/E basis, risk appetite is returning, and the search for yield is back on.

Own assets or be left behind.

items.title: BREAKING: The US is considering a "$20 billion cash-for-uranium deal" with Iran as part of a new "3 page peace plan," per Axios. Details include: 1. T...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: The US is considering a "$20 billion cash-for-uranium deal" with Iran as part of a new "3 page peace plan," per Axios.

Details include:

1. The US and Iran are negotiating over a 3-page plan to end the war

2. One element under discussion is that the US would release $20 billion in frozen Iranian funds in return for Iran giving up its stockpile of enriched uranium

3. Some of Iran's highly enriched uranium would be shipped to a third country and some would be down-blended in Iran under international monitoring

4. Iran would be allowed to have nuclear research reactors for the production of medical isotopes, but would pledge that all of its nuclear facilities would be above ground

A second round of US-Iran talks is expected in Pakistan on Sunday.

items.title: Good way of looking at opportunity size, Case study by Ramesh Damani Sir - "It comes from an understanding of what you are investing in, rather than i...
items.description_html:
Good way of looking at opportunity size, Case study by Ramesh Damani Sir -

"It comes from an understanding of what you are investing in, rather than investing in the price action. That is the difference between fundamentalists and price action-oriented investors. An investor who is price action-oriented will think that the stock has tripled, so let me take some money home.

While the fundamentalist will understand that he has bought something that has deep value and even if the stock has tripled, it doesn’t mean that the value has been extracted.

Let me give you an example from my life to illustrate the point. In 2003-04, when I was trying to look for new themes, I realized that alcoholic beverage stocks were very cheap. You could have bought 50% of India’s liquor industry for a few hundred crores.

It’s staggering to think that - in a country with a 1 + billion population, with no religious inhibition about drinking, and where people are young and getting into drinking age. With Rs. 100 crore, you could have bought 10-20% in the Indian liquor industry or 2 flats in NCPA in Mumbai - that was the value proposition.

You could have seen what Anheuser-Busch is valued at in America and what San Miguel is valued at in the Philippines. Then if you bought with conviction, you would not sell even if it became Rs. 400 crore or Rs. 1000 crore, because that’s silly. It could be valued at Rs. 50,000 crore or Rs. 100,000 crore, or maybe there is no terminal value.

A true fundamentalist will figure that equation out, without knowing the exact timeframes. These just become a core part of the portfolio. That’s the difference between a true value investor, a fundamentalist, and a price action person.

That is where it is important to have a grounding in what you buy. I hung around with a group of people who were very deep-value investors, and that helped me immeasurably in life, because I have seen how they have done it. I have seen with my own eyes how Gillette shares were bought at Rs. 45. The share price went up to Rs. 100 and then Rs. 500, and not even 100 shares were sold, still sitting tight with it. It helps to have a good network."

From the Book Masterclass with Super Investors; no stock reco

items.title: One has to think - the transformer paper, ChatGPT, Claude, and now Mythos - all are from the US, not China. The framing that Silicon Valley aims for t...
items.description_html:
One has to think - the transformer paper, ChatGPT, Claude, and now Mythos - all are from the US, not China.

The framing that Silicon Valley aims for the sky while China aims for making concrete AI products doesn't make sense to me.

items.title: Did you know why Nuclear energy is making a comeback? One kilogram of enriched uranium (4% U-235) produces roughly 160,000 kWh of electricity. For com...
items.description_html:
Did you know why Nuclear energy is making a comeback?

One kilogram of enriched uranium (4% U-235) produces roughly 160,000 kWh of electricity. For comparison:

1 kg of coal → 3 kWh
1 kg of natural gas → 15 kWh
1 kg of diesel → 12 kWh

This is not a marginal improvement. It is a 50,000x advantage over coal by mass. It's why one standard 700 MWe PHWR in India replaces a 2,800 MW coal plant equivalent on annual energy.

items.title: Mastek delivered strong PAT growth of 30.9% YoY driven by UK & Europe performance (15.2% revenue growth, 400bps margin expansion) and significantly lo...
items.description_html:
Mastek delivered strong PAT growth of 30.9% YoY driven by UK & Europe performance (15.2% revenue growth, 400bps margin expansion) and significantly lower tax rate. However, the company missed its EBITDA margin target (15.8% vs 16.5-17.0% guidance) and US business continues to deteriorate.

Cash generation was exceptional with 1.34x cash conversion and net cash position of ₹282.94 Crore. Growth has decelerated significantly (7.0% FY26 vs 13.1% FY25) and geographic concentration risk increased with UK now representing 66.4% of revenue.

Positive Factors

PAT grew 30.9% YoY to ₹106.15 Crore in Q4, normalized PAT up 42.0%

UK & Europe revenue grew 15.2% YoY with 400 bps margin expansion to 20.2%

Operating cash flow strong at ₹541.69 Crore with 1.34x cash conversion

Net cash position of ₹282.94 Crore achieved, debt reduced 24.1%

Total dividend ₹24 per share for FY26 (interim ₹8 + final ₹16)

Total equity grew 21.5% YoY to ₹2,991.85 Crore

Risk Factors

US business revenue declined 14.5% YoY, segment margin collapsed to 5.3%

FY26 revenue growth of 7.0% decelerated from 13.1% in FY25

EBITDA margin 16.1% (Q4) and 15.8% (FY) below 16.5-17.0% target

UK & Europe concentration risk increased to 66.4% of revenue

AMEA revenue declined 11.9% YoY with margin at 4.3%

items.title: BAJAJCON delivered exceptional Q4 FY26 results with revenue beating management's 10% guidance by 5.8 percentage points and EBITDA margin inflecting to...
items.description_html:
BAJAJCON delivered exceptional Q4 FY26 results with revenue beating management's 10% guidance by 5.8 percentage points and EBITDA margin inflecting to 23.4% (up 1089 bps YoY). PAT grew 105% YoY with superior cash conversion (1.04x CFO/PAT).

The company achieved its medium-term margin aspiration on a quarterly run-rate basis while maintaining strong operating leverage. Share buyback of ₹19,019.82 Lakh demonstrates efficient capital allocation. International subsidiary losses remain a concern but do not offset the strong domestic performance.

Positive Factors

FY26 standalone revenue grew 15.8% YoY, beating management guidance of 10%

Q4 EBITDA margin reached 23.4%, up 1089 bps YoY

PAT grew 105.25% YoY to ₹6,359.57 Lakh in Q4

EPS grew 113% YoY to ₹4.75 in Q4

CFO to PAT conversion ratio at 1.04x indicating high cash quality

Operating leverage strong with expenses up 15.2% vs revenue up 30.4% YoY

Share buyback of ₹19,019.82 Lakh returned capital to shareholders

Company remains debt-free with strong balance sheet

Risk Factors

International subsidiary business reported net loss of ₹355 Lakh for FY26

IndAS 116 lease capitalization artificially boosted reported EBITDA

VPCL acquisition in May 2025 affects comparability with prior year

Demand environment in rural areas remains challenging with 600-700 bps gap vs urban

items.title: Angel One quietly became one of the best performing stocks in the Capital Markets space over the last 1 M Delivered a strong recovery quarter with mar...
items.description_html:
Angel One quietly became one of the best performing stocks in the Capital Markets space over the last 1 M

Delivered a strong recovery quarter with margins improving meaningfully

The context is important to understand. Broking businesses had already gone through 3 consecutive quarters of de-growth, as 2025 remained a muted year which impacted volumes. This was further affected by regulatory changes & True to Label framework

This quarter marks the return of REV growth, largely supported by a low base

Emerging businesses continue to scale steadily & are becoming increasingly relevant to the overall business mix.

Wealth management crossed 10,080 Cr
Client funding book also remains healthy, with the average client funding book at 5,850 Cr

Margins are returning to the guided range & PAT has reached a multi-quarter high

Order activity also showed encouraging signs of recovery. A six quarter high in order volumes was recorded in Q4. Average daily orders improved from the lows of around 5 Mn to 7.4 Mn by March

As market conditions continue to improve, operating momentum is expected to accelerate further from here

Emerging businesses across credit, MF, wealth & AMC continue to gain traction with increasing adoption from the existing client base

items.title: CRISIL delivered strong Q1 FY26 with consolidated revenue up 30.1% YoY and PAT up 45.9% YoY, accelerating significantly from 11.9% revenue growth in F...
items.description_html:
CRISIL delivered strong Q1 FY26 with consolidated revenue up 30.1% YoY and PAT up 45.9% YoY, accelerating significantly from 11.9% revenue growth in FY25. Both segments showed robust performance with Ratings margin at 50.5% and Research growing 34.9% YoY with 440bps margin expansion.

Operating leverage is evident as expenses grew slower than revenue. Key risk is foreign exchange translation loss of ₹50.31 crore and standalone PBT decline of 15% YoY. No specific guidance provided for FY26.

Positive Factors

Consolidated revenue grew 30.1% YoY to ₹1,057.66 crore, accelerating from 11.9% in FY25

Consolidated PAT grew 45.9% YoY to ₹233.26 crore, outpacing revenue growth

EBITDA margin expanded 157bps YoY to 30.1%

Ratings segment EBIT margin reached 50.5%, up 80bps YoY with 890bps sequential improvement

Research segment revenue grew 34.9% YoY with EBIT up 66.9%

Subsidiaries now contribute 55.1% of consolidated revenue and 51.4% of PAT

Management priorities of revenue acceleration and margin expansion confirmed achieved

Risk Factors

Standalone PAT declined 12.6% YoY vs consolidated PAT growth of 45.9%

Foreign exchange translation loss of ₹50.31 crore in OCI vs ₹17.31 crore prior year

Professional fees jumped 60% YoY in consolidated, 49% in standalone

Sequential revenue declined 2.2% QoQ due to seasonality

Tax disputes of ₹148 crore remain as contingent liability

items.title: MBAPL delivered a major revenue surprise in FY26 with 76.3% growth far exceeding the 15% guidance, but execution quality is mixed as EBITDA and gross ...
items.description_html:
MBAPL delivered a major revenue surprise in FY26 with 76.3% growth far exceeding the 15% guidance, but execution quality is mixed as EBITDA and gross margins both missed guidance.

The company is aggressively funding capacity expansion with 169% debt growth, resulting in a highly leveraged balance sheet (D/E 1.56x) and deeply negative free cash flow of ₹526 Cr. While revenue performance is exceptional, the cash conversion crisis and margin compression present significant risks that offset the top-line strength.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 76.3% YoY to ₹1,866.98 Cr, beating
management guidance of 15% by 61.3 percentage points

FY26 PAT grew 161.3% YoY to ₹150.18 Cr (reported) or 91.1% YoY normalized

EBITDA grew 55.0% YoY to ₹225.51 Cr

Capex of ₹398.5 Cr executed as planned, CWIP grew 274% to ₹277.5 Cr indicating capacity expansion underway

Total assets grew 77.9% YoY to ₹1,682.4 Cr, showing significant balance sheet expansion

Q4 PAT grew 319.3% YoY driven by strong operational performance and tax adjustment

Risk Factors

Operating cash flow turned deeply negative at ₹(127.4) Cr due to ₹333.1 Cr working capital buildup

Free cash flow highly negative at ₹(525.9) Cr, funded entirely by ₹537 Cr new debt

Total borrowings surged 169% YoY to ₹855.4 Cr, Debt/Equity doubled from 0.79x to 1.56x

EBITDA margin contracted 165 bps to 12.1% vs guidance of 13.7%

Gross margin missed guidance at 26.6% vs target ~30%

Trade receivables up 66% YoY to ₹426.3 Cr and inventories up 115.7% to ₹360.2 Cr

items.title: ASML spent 20 years and billions of dollars on tech that "would never work." The problem? EUV light is so extreme that: • Air absorbs it • Glass len...
items.description_html:
ASML spent 20 years and billions of dollars on tech that "would never work."

The problem? EUV light is so extreme that:
• Air absorbs it
• Glass lenses can't handle it
• A single dust particle ruins the process

Every optical system had to be reinvented from scratch.

Competitors gave up. ASML didn't.

By 2018, no chip on earth could be made without their machine.

The lesson: the harder the engineering problem, the wider the moat.

items.title: He is so wrong on this. AI will certainly cause dislocation, but like all technology it will also create new jobs and opportunities in the medium term...
items.description_html:
He is so wrong on this. AI will certainly cause dislocation, but like all technology it will also create new jobs and opportunities in the medium term. AI and robots will also not produce goods and services in excess of money or demand that there will be no inflation. Both of these are classic mistakes made by those who think that there is a finite number of jobs to be done in the world and a finite set of consumer demands. By their logic, we have already exceeded everything that even the wealthiest person could have imagined in 1800, so there should be no jobs or inflation in the 21st century. By the same token, @elonmusk 's universal high income will bankrupt any government that attempts it.


Elon Musk: Universal HIGH INCOME via checks issued by the Federal government is the best way to deal with unemployment caused by AI.

AI/robotics will produce goods & services far in excess of the increase in the money supply, so there will not be inflation.

items.title: RT Intrinsic Compounding: In FY24, buried inside L&T’s Heavy Engineering segment was a quiet NPCIL order for nuclear reactor components. No press rel...
items.description_html:
RT Intrinsic Compounding
In FY24, buried inside L&T’s Heavy Engineering segment was a quiet NPCIL order for nuclear reactor components.

No press release. No analyst note. Just a footnote in a business that has been supplying India's nuclear program for three decades — with approvals that take 7+ years to earn and face almost zero competition once granted.

Most investors missed it.

But India’s nuclear energy sector is at an inflection point:
⚡ ₹20,000 Cr Nuclear Mission (Budget Announcement)
⚡ Policy changes to allow private capital
⚡ Target: 100 GW by 2047 (vs ~8 GW today)

This isn’t just energy. This could be a multi-decade capex story where the real winners may be the qualified suppliers few are tracking.

This Sunday, we decode it from an investing lens.

items.description_html:
While most Indian households now own a mobile phone, computer ownership in India has grown slowly. Over the last two decades, the share of households that own a computer has increased from just over 1% to just over 9%. In most developed countries, by contrast, computer ownership exceeds 75%.

Among the 30 million households in India that own a computer, 90% have a laptop, while 20% own a desktop. A little under 10% of computer-owning households report having both a laptop and a desktop.

India has also made limited progress in the ability to use computers over the last decade. A little over one in five adults, or 200 million people, were able to operate a computer as of 2025. This share is roughly twice the share of households that own a computer, suggesting that many people may have learned to use a computer, or use one in college or at work, without owning one themselves.

While the proportion of rural adults who can use a computer has increased, it still stands at just about one in six. Among urban adults, the figure is much higher with over one in three adults, but it has grown only marginally over the last decade.

How does the ability to use computers vary by age group and gender in India? Read @akwaghmare’s piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/computers/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Computer #Technology #Laptop #India #DataForIndia

items.title: RT menaka doshi: In all the politics over Lok Sabha seats an important point deserves attention. India's constitution links delimitation to population...
items.description_html:
RT menaka doshi
In all the politics over Lok Sabha seats an important point deserves attention.

India's constitution links delimitation to population.
But does that create the right development incentives for states?

"A formulaic, population-based approach in one of the most unequal, federal democracies in the world is a flawed approach. Beyond the immediate inequities, such seat distribution would not offer states the right incentives and rewards for development."

- Praveen Chakravarty, Congress Party @pravchak

"There is a tension between the democratic impulse that the vote of every citizen should count equally and the federal impulse that all states should have equal stakes in the national polity.

To use a phrase from economics, there is no corner solution. States and Center will have to find a modus vivendi involving seat adjustments in Lok Sabha and strengthening of other institutions envisaged by the Constitution for protecting state interests such as Rajya Sabha."

- Avinash M Tripathi, Center for Study of Indian Economy, Azim Premji University
@qfint

"Sticking to the original constitutional position of one person, one vote, one value, will allow the northern states to dominate the Indian polity and leave the south feeling powerless. An imperfect union can survive but an unjust union won’t."

- Sanjay Hegde, Senior Advocate
@sanjayuvacha

Read 👇
https://www.bloomberg.com/news/newsletters/2026-04-16/india-s-modi-uses-women-s-quota-to-remake-parliament-with-delimitation-push

items.title: RT ValueEquity: New series : Learnings from what I heard today I was listening to the founders podcast on Rockefeller and Larry Ellison again, both ab...
items.description_html:
RT ValueEquity
New series : Learnings from what I heard today

I was listening to the founders podcast on Rockefeller and Larry Ellison again, both absolute legendary risk takers and have changed the way that business has been done and shaped many companies that we see today

Some Common things I found Both Standard Oil and Oracle do

1) Huge focus on costs , how does the company in a cyclical industry ( Standard Oil ) make cost a fix expense across the cycle and capture as many parts of the value chain they can . Efficiency was his religion

Standard Oil wanted rebates from the rail roads , and control across every part of the value chain , he realized his priority should be his biggest cost center , and that for them was logistics , from mapping the location of the plants to making a commission on every barrel of oil sold , he figured that efficiency drives profits , and Standard Oil is one of the living examples of a hugely profitable company since inception , He always believed in reinvesting aggressively when others took profits

2) Be aggressively contrarian (but right)

Ellison repeatedly emphasized

You don’t get rich doing what everyone else is doing.

He deliberately chose paths others thought were wrong, but kept backing them

relational databases (before mainstream adoption)
network computing vs PCs
enterprise stack integration
cloud positioning later vs peers

He said he’s happiest when people think he’s wrong
because asymmetric opportunities live there.

3) What I really liked about this strategy from Oracle and Larry was picking a giant enemy , picking Microsoft as an enemy knowing that they were in a different league actually motivated the folks to push harder , and he believed in self and marketing , it helped him a long way

You all should check out the founders podcast , Editions of Larry and Rockefeller on Youtube , I listen to it on my runs !

items.title: RT John Kemp: Europe’s gas prices retreat on greater confidence about refill Europe’s gas futures prices have retreated to the lowest since the Unit...
items.description_html:
RT John Kemp
Europe’s gas prices retreat on greater confidence about refill

Europe’s gas futures prices have retreated to the lowest since the United States and Israel attacked Iran, prompting closure of the Strait of Hormuz and halting gas exports from Qatar and the United Arab Emirates.

Since the middle of March, traders have become more confident Europe will be able to secure enough LNG to refill storage without another surge in prices.

[This is a short extract from my newsletter emailed to clients earlier today. If you would like to read my newsletters in full, three times a week, you can subscribe via my website https://newsletter.jkempenergy.com/gUJLdm ]

items.title: In 2006, while his Matrix Laboratories colleagues toasted their $736M acquisition(one of the largest pharma deals in Indian history), Satyanarayana Ch...
items.description_html:
In 2006, while his Matrix Laboratories colleagues toasted their $736M acquisition(one of the largest pharma deals in Indian history), Satyanarayana Chava was sketching molecules on a napkin.

He quit Matrix Laboratories as their CTO and started Laurus Labs with personal savings.

No VC. No corporate backing.

The Indian pharma industry was a fast-follower game — copy patents, race to market.

He wanted to build something that actually solved problems.

The timing? Brutal. Chinese manufacturers were dumping APIs at prices below raw material cost.

He did it anyway. Laurus Labs is worth ₹61,000 Cr today. The best founders don't wait for the right moment. They create it.

no reco

items.title: Paytm owner One97 Communications has now become an Indian-owned and controlled company (IOCC). Until now, IOCC status has been significant for online ...
items.description_html:
Paytm owner One97 Communications has now become an Indian-owned and controlled company (IOCC).

Until now, IOCC status has been significant for online retailers, as it allows them to hold inventory.

Eternal-owned Blinkit became one through a QIP, Swiggy is close to getting there, and Nykaa has always had this advantage.

Paytm is among the first major listed fintechs to get IOCC status.

There may not be immediate, direct business benefits, but this is important given the scrutiny Paytm has faced over ownership by China’s Alibaba and Ant Financial, which together held 38% before the IPO.

After the IPO in 2021, foreign investors held 81%, which has now come down to just below 50%.

items.title: Updates on GLP 1 market in India CareEdge Ratings estimates that India’s potential GLP‑1 market is set to expand nearly fivefold, from Rs 1,000–Rs ...
items.description_html:
Updates on GLP 1 market in India

CareEdge Ratings estimates that India’s potential GLP‑1 market is set to expand nearly fivefold, from Rs 1,000–Rs 1,200 crore in 2025 to approximately Rs 4,500–5,000 crore by 2030.

Post patent expiry, drug prices are expected to decline by around 40–50% in FY27, followed by a further reduction of 10–30% in FY28 due to heightened competition.

Within 10 days of launch, generic semaglutide has added 40k new patients & already taken 24% value share of India’s sema mkt, with overall vols up 2.5X.

Eli Lilly's Mounjaro (tirzepatide), a competitor to semaglutide, became India's highest-selling drug by value and has overtaken Augmentin by GSK.

items.title: RT Andy Mok - China | Frontier Tech | Governance: Re Great thread. The real question isn’t whether the finance is available, it’s why it isn’t. And...
items.description_html:
RT Andy Mok - China | Frontier Tech | Governance
Re Great thread. The real question isn’t whether the finance is available, it’s why it isn’t. And the answer is governance.

China’s system is built for directed coordination: identify a destination, align institutions, create demand, scale. That works brilliantly for EVs, digital payments, semiconductors.

But AGI requires a different kind of capital, one that tolerates open-ended, speculative risk with no clear destination.

That capital ecosystem is itself a governance product, and it’s one the US system generates and China’s doesn’t.

This is the framework I explore in my book, The Innovation Machine (Springer Nature, 2026). Export controls didn’t touch this dynamic. What they did is push semiconductors into the category where China’s governance model thrives: clear target, state mandate, institutional competition.

The US created exactly the competitor it didn’t need to.

https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-981-95-7056-0

items.title: RT Arnaud Bertrand: I rarely have reasons to be proud of France these days, but this is definitely one. France's parliament just voted - unanimously, ...
items.description_html:
RT Arnaud Bertrand
I rarely have reasons to be proud of France these days, but this is definitely one.

France's parliament just voted - unanimously, 170 votes to 0 - a law that institutionalizes the restitution of cultural artifacts looted during the colonial era (the law covers a massive 157-year period).

It's going absolutely viral in Chinese social media because of this speech 👇 by MP @JPatrierLeitus who noted in Parliament that it included items stolen to China during the joint British-French sack of the Summer Palace in 1860.

Patrier-Leitus cites Victor Hugo's famous 1861 letter to Captain Butler, the British officer who wrote to him seeking his endorsement of the expedition - and got the exact opposite.

Hugo wrote (whole letter here: https://www.yuanmingyuan.eu/en/the-looting/victor-hugo-letter/):

"One day two bandits entered the Summer Palace. One plundered, the other burned. Victory can be a thieving woman, or so it seems. The devastation of the Summer Palace was accomplished by the two victors acting jointly. Mixed up in all this is the name of Elgin, which inevitably calls to mind the Parthenon. What was done to the Parthenon was done to the Summer Palace, more thoroughly and better, so that nothing of it should be left. All the treasures of all our cathedrals put together could not equal this formidable and splendid museum of the Orient. It contained not only masterpieces of art, but masses of jewelry. What a great exploit, what a windfall! One of the two victors filled his pockets; when the other saw this he filled his coffers. And back they came to Europe, arm in arm, laughing away. Such is the story of the two bandits.

We Europeans are the civilized ones, and for us the Chinese are the barbarians. This is what civilization has done to barbarism.

Before history, one of the two bandits will be called France; the other will be called England. But I protest, and I thank you for giving me the opportunity! the crimes of those who lead are not the fault of those who are led; Governments are sometimes bandits, peoples never.

The French empire has pocketed half of this victory, and today with a kind of proprietorial naivety it displays the splendid bric-a-brac of the Summer Palace. I hope that a day will come when France, delivered and cleansed, will return this booty to despoiled China.

Meanwhile, there is a theft and two thieves. I take note.

This, Sir, is how much approval I give to the China expedition."

Hugo's letter is so revered in China that a bronze bust of him stands today at the Summer Palace ruins - I believe the only instance of a Westerner honored in China at the site of his own country's crime.

A powerful testament of how much a single act of intellectual honesty can redeem, if not a nation, then at least a name.

Hugo was also prescient: as Patrier-Leitus notes, that day "when France, delivered and cleansed, will return this booty to despoiled China" has indeed come (even though the "delivered and cleansed" part is, overall, pretty questionable in the current context).

This new law doesn't only concern China and the Summer Palace: it concerns ALL stolen artifacts by France during the period ranging between November 1815 and April 1972 - corresponding to the start of the second French colonial empire to the entry into force of the UNESCO convention on cultural property.

It's a massive scope: 157 years, thousands of objects and dozens of nations with potential claims. It's France reckoning with its colonial past in an unprecedented way and the fact ALL of France's MPs voted in favor of the law, without a single exception, is also pretty remarkable.

Hopefully this will also serve as a signal to other countries, especially the UK - the other "bandit" in Hugo's letter.

There is this Chinese saying from the Zuo Zhuan (左传), one of the foundational Confucian classics:

"To err and be able to correct it - there is no greater virtue." ("过而能改，善莫大焉", "guò ér néng gǎi, shàn mò dà yān").

France, with this law, proved its virtue.


Jérémie Patrier-Leitus: « Un jour viendra où la France, délivrée et nettoyée, renverra ce butin à la Chine pillée. »
Ce jour, qu’appelait Victor Hugo de ses vœux en 1861, est venu.
🇫🇷Cette loi vient inscrire dans notre droit un cadre clair, cohérent et précis pour la restitution des biens culturels.

Mark Mobius, who championed emerging markets, long before it became fashionable to do, has passed away.

Mobius was was known for his hands-on, on-the-ground investment philosophy. Over the course of his career, he visited over a 100 countries in search of undervalued companies.

Mobius retired from Franklin Templeton in 2018 after more than three decades with the firm - during which period - Templeton's EM assets went from $100m to $400B.

I have been speaking with Mark for over 20 years & he was always polite, generous with his time & insights. He remained a firm believer in the India story - both the economy & it's companies.

Rest well, Mark ! @CNBCTV18News

items.title: RT Rui Ma: I just listened to Dwarkesh interview of Jensen where he asked about export controls. Jensen said that Anthropic Mythos was trained on a mu...
items.description_html:
RT Rui Ma
I just listened to Dwarkesh interview of Jensen where he asked about export controls. Jensen said that Anthropic Mythos was trained on a mundane number of chips and that China has no shortage of such capacity plus a lot of high end talent. The conclusion being that research should be globally collaborative and not antagonistic or siloed.

My understanding of Chinese AI is somewhat complementary. There are chips. But they are expensive. Chinese investors are unwilling to fund experiments into AGI / ASI. There are only like 2 or 3 commercial players that the industry generally and truly believes with such as their goal. A few others may state they want to do it, but are hamstrung by their ability to fundraise from the market (a much smaller pool of capital vs US companies) or their boards. Maybe sounds silly to some folks outside of China, but shouldn’t to those who have followed me for a long time. I’ve written extensively about how finance shapes innovation, and how there is, relatively speaking, a notable disparity in availability of truly risk-taking capital in the private markets.

I just don’t think there is much evidence export controls are a main reason for the successes or failures of AI development in China. What export controls very clearly did though is give the Chinese semiconductor sector a huge boost. And in that I think people really underestimate the competitor that has been created, which was entirely unnecessary. But whatever, probably good for humanity overall to have diversification for such a critical technology. But US really could have monopolized that for the foreseeable future.

They want to fell 521 trees on Ganeshkhind Road. Please sign the objection email on Ganeshkhind Road Trees 🌳.

the problem in a nutshell; this road widening is just going to cause pools of traffic and congestion due to the bottlenecks superimposed by the metro pillars and stations placed at 36 metres over the road widening to 45 metres. It’s a stupid X 3 decision and at the cost of over 521 old growth trees along Ganeshkhind Road.

45 metres from 36 metres will mean only 5 metres increase on either side. If the footpaths take away some space there is literally no lane left to accomodate any more vehicles. How is a 2 metre or a 2.5 road lane expansion justifying the massacre of so many ancient trees ?

Because, The application of mind to save these trees simply doesn’t exist.

items.title: RT Brian Albrecht: Dwarkesh asks what's actually bottlenecking AI capacity right now. Huang's first answer isn't fabs. Isn't memory. Isn't power. Isn'...
items.description_html:
RT Brian Albrecht
Dwarkesh asks what's actually bottlenecking AI capacity right now.

Huang's first answer isn't fabs. Isn't memory. Isn't power. Isn't even regulation. It's plumbers and electricians.

I think Huang is more in line with how economists think about this than how AI people do.

In the paper we talked about how the binding constraint in chipmaking keeps moving. Fix CoWoS packaging, it shows up as HBM supply. Fix HBM, it's power. Fix power, it's tradesmen.

Already quoted, Huang's own story about CoWoS earlier in the interview is the same shape: "for two years we swarmed the living daylights out of it." That bottleneck is gone. Another one took its place.

This is also why I keep pushing back on the labor-share-goes-to-zero story, including @dwarkesh_sp https://www.economicforces.xyz/p/ai-labor-share?utm_source=publication-search

When AI-capital becomes abundant, the complements get scarce. The complements earn the rents. The scare resource earns the rents.

Right now one of those complements is a guy with a conduit bender. For as far as the eye can see, there is some constraint.


Dwarkesh Patel: The Jensen Huang episode.

0:00:00 – Is Nvidia’s biggest moat its grip on scarce supply chains?
0:16:25 – Will TPUs break Nvidia’s hold on AI compute?
0:41:06 – Why doesn’t Nvidia become a hyperscaler?
0:57:36 – Should we be selling AI chips to China?
1:35:06 – Why doesn’t Nvidia

Most investors are still thinking metals the old way:
Demand up → prices up → producers win.

That model is decaying.
The metals play, especially the aluminium market, is shifting toward recycling-led supply - for good reasons, it consumes 95% less energy.

As metals move from extraction to recycling, the value pool shifts from producing metal to fixing metals - the Recylcing and enablers.

items.title: #LaurusLabs, at today’s not-so-cheap valuations and hovering near its all-time highs, is not a HOLD if your view is just 3 months. But stretch that v...
items.description_html:
#LaurusLabs, at today’s not-so-cheap valuations and hovering near its all-time highs, is not a HOLD if your view is just 3 months. But stretch that view to 3 years, and it still might be.

This gap comes down to time. As Howard Marks and Nassim Taleb remind us, risk and value shift with the clock. What feels expensive and fragile in the short run can turn reasonable once time allows the business to prove itself.

In 'Fooled by Randomness', Taleb tells a story about a retired dentist who is a skilled investor. The dentist expects to earn about 15% more than safe government bonds each year. But he checks his portfolio many times a day. This habit causes stress because, in the short term, prices move almost randomly. Taleb calls this “noise”, which simply means meaningless fluctuations that do not reflect real value.

Over very short periods, like minutes or days, the chance of seeing a gain is barely above 50%. This means that over three months, price movements are driven mostly by luck and maybe Q4 FY26 numbers or, rather, the interpretation of those. If a stock is already “not-so-cheap”, its price reflects a lot of optimism. There is little room for disappointment. Even a small negative surprise can push the price down. It's always albout the rate of change relative to the asking run-rate (valuations)

In 'Most Important Thing', Marks explains that risk often comes from paying too high a price. When you buy an expensive stock, you have almost no “margin for error”. This simply means you have little room to be wrong and still be okay. In the short term, bad luck or shifting sentiment can cause sharp falls. He also points out that being early can feel the same as being wrong, especially over short periods.

Taleb offers another example in 'Fooled by Randomness'. He describes the idea of building a ski resort in Miami. Imagine a rare blizzard hits, and the resort makes a huge profit in its first year. That success does not make it a good decision. It was simply luck. In the same way, making money on an expensive stock over 3 months may just mean you were lucky, not right.

Things look different over longer periods. As time passes, real business performance begins to matter more than daily price swings. Taleb notes that over a year, the odds of seeing gains improve greatly. Over 3 years, the company’s actual progress has a much better chance to show through the noise. What the business earns and builds starts to matter more than short-term sentiment.

Back in 2023, I was one of the few bullish voices on Laurus. Talk is cheap, so I backed that view with my own money. Sell ratings were common at the time, and many were anchored to targets around 250, which weighed heavily on sentiment.

Marks explains that while prices can drift in the short run, terminal value pulls them back over time. Terminal value is simply what a business is truly worth based on what it can earn and build.

For Laurus Labs, the CDMO business is that anchor. Over the next five to seven years, this segment alone could cross 10,000 Cr. in annual sales. Then, post-2033, the precision fermentation and bio-engine should be all guns blazing.

If a company grows well, it can “grow into” a high MCap. This is why a stock that looks expensive today may still work out over a few years. Over 3 years, a strong business may justify a high starting price. Over 3 months, you are mostly exposed to chance.

Taleb compares short-term risk to a version of Russian roulette with many chambers. You might avoid losses several times, but the risk is always there. Without a margin of safety, a sudden drop can happen at any time. This is why short-term investing in expensive stocks can feel unpredictable and fragile.

In the end, time changes the nature of the bet. Over 3 months, randomness dominates, and a high price leaves little protection. Over 3 years, business performance has time to come through, and value can catch up with price. The same stock can look risky or reasonable depending on how long you plan to hold it.

Laurus Labs is our core family holding, and none of this is an investment recommendation.

items.title: Technicals for power transmission space are turning back... The fundamental view point for the same:- India's power sector is entering a multi-year ex...
items.description_html:
Technicals for power transmission space are turning back...

The fundamental view point for the same:-

India's power sector is entering a multi-year expansion supercycle, shifting from an installed base of ~525 GW to over 800 GW. While the long-term narrative heavily favors renewables, near-term capital is flowing predominantly toward conventional thermal and transmission due to physical grid constraints and baseload realities

India ranks #3 globally in renewable additions (~45 GW added last year). The aggressive runway targets 50 GW/year over the next 5 years (~250 GW total). In contrast, conventional thermal power will add a steady 8-10 GW/year, led primarily by NTPC and Adani Power

The Unit Economics & Capex Math -

1) Solar: ~₹5 Cr/MW with a 1-2 year build time. India boasts a Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) of ~$30/MWh (₹2.5/unit), making it one of the cheapest globally due to high solar irradiance
2) Thermal (Coal): ~₹15 Cr/MW with a 5-year build time for greenfield projects
3) Nuclear: ~₹25 Cr/MW with a rigid 10-year build time. France serves as a historical parallel for India's nuclear ambitions, with long-term goals scaling from the current 8 GW to 100 GW by 2047

There is an under penetration in electrification currently, electricity comprises only ~18% of India's total energy mix, compared to ~30% in China and the low-20s globally. To achieve energy self-sufficiency amidst geopolitical volatility, power demand growth is projected to stabilize at a 1x multiplier to real GDP (~6% long-term growth)

Coming to a big tailwind of data centre capex, Current capacity sits at 1.5 GW, but is estimated to ramp up to 5-8 GW by 2030, driven by favorable grid access and clean energy mandates. This will incrementally consume ~2% of India’s overall electricity mix.

But what is the primary bottleneck with all this here? Transmission capacity… While India operates an efficient synchronous grid, a solar plant takes 1 year to build, whereas high-voltage transmission lines require ~3 years due to land acquisition and permitting. This lag will inevitably cap the realistic absorption of new solar capacity.

There might be a case where pure play renewable power generators might be in headwinds due to margin pressures and transmission bottlenecks which will make a investment case a little stronger for conventional power generators, power transmission value chain, new power infra such as nuclear energy and others and high quality/ top-tier EPC players.

Just quick note on few of the industry giants on this value chain -

1) NTPC - Operating as a highly defensive core holding. NTPC is leading the thermal capacity charge (17 GW under construction), spearheading future nuclear JVs, and incubating what will likely become India's largest renewable subsidiary

2) Power Grid - A direct beneficiary of the electrification and transmission theme, though near-term execution has been dragged by land acquisition hurdles. This one player drives compelete transmission value chain in the country

3) Larsen & Tubro - Largest EPC player present in multiple parts of the power value chain. The fundamental metrics are incredibly solid where ROEs are inching toward 17-19% with high-teens EPS visibility. However, the stock currently trades as a call on geopolitical stability, given that the Middle East accounts for a massive 37% of its order book.

4) PFC & REC - Their historical rerating was driven by the broad power theme, but loan book growth has fundamentally decelerated as Discom health improves and tier-1 banks chip away at market share. However, a potential merger between the two would be highly accretive—eliminating the ~50% holdco discount for PFC and forging a top-10 profitable Indian entity

5) Solar PV Manufacturers - Massive supply-side additions for solar cell manufacturing are coming online, which, coupled with transmission-capped demand, creates a high probability of margin compression over the next 12 months

6) IEX - Despite bottom-barrel valuations, structural and regulatory overhangs persist. Aside from the threat of market coupling, the exchange's transaction fees (~4 paise) are nearly 5x the global average (~1%), creating sustained headline risks

Disclaimer - not a recommendation to buy/sell

Custome index chart - https://www.stockscans.in/charts/PCI:TRANSMISSION (@stockscansin)

items.title: Why 27th April is imp for Iran ? Iran’s oil system is built for flow—not storage. A) It produces ~3.2 million barrels per day, consumes ~1.7 million...
items.description_html:
Why 27th April is imp for Iran ?

Iran’s oil system is built for flow—not storage.

A) It produces ~3.2 million barrels per day, consumes ~1.7 million, leaving roughly 1.5 million barrels per day with nowhere to go if exports are disrupted.

B) Onshore storage sits around 50–55 million barrels and is already ~60% full. That leaves ~20 million barrels of headroom—barely two weeks of buffer.

C) After that, Iran doesn’t just lose exports—it faces a harder choice: shut in wells.

And in mature fields, shutting in production isn’t reversible like flipping a switch. Water intrusion can damage reservoirs, meaning when wells restart, they produce less oil—and sometimes mostly water.

Estimates suggest prolonged shut-ins could risk hundreds of thousands of barrels per day in lasting production loss.

This isn’t just a supply disruption. It’s potential supply destruction.

@FinancialTimes @meghal_talati


Yatin Mota: Iran Faces Halt To Oil Production Within Weeks If US Blockade Succeeds – FT

https://www.ft.com/content/dec9c510-9dd8-434b-abe8-5f9ecbb41e55

items.title: When did Mr Ramesh Damani Sell Infosys, and what was the thought process behind selling it? His words - "When we saw that the 2000 TMT (Technology, Me...
items.description_html:
When did Mr Ramesh Damani Sell Infosys, and what was the thought process behind selling it?

His words -

"When we saw that the 2000 TMT (Technology, Media, Telecom) bull market was getting over, I realized that these values wouldn’t hold.

Infosys was trading at 150+ forward P/E. Even if you try to hold the stock, the markets rattle you out of the position.

Infosys went up three straight circuits to Rs. 13,000 and then started falling. So when it fell to Rs. 8000-10,000 levels, I sold it, as I could not handle the stress of holding it since so much money was involved.

I had realized that the bull market was over. I had learnt that when a bull market ends, the leaders are squashed completely. So I did not want to hold the stock or even buy it back. When the stock fell to Rs. 3000, I did not buy because I knew that the new markets would have new leaders. So I just kept the cash for some time."

From the Book Masterclass with Super Investors

items.title: "The opportunities are fairly large in the world market today. Earlier, India used to be a low-cost country for sourcing for the world market. But wha...
items.description_html:
"The opportunities are fairly large in the world market today. Earlier, India used to be a low-cost country for sourcing for the world market.

But what we have done, what we are doing, is actually IP-driven products. So, there's a shift in what we do and what is typically imported, especially in the defence scenario

The spending in the European market is going to go up due to the geopolitical environment. I think there's an opportunity for us, companies like us, who have strong IP-driven product development capabilities, to participate and address some of the needs where they will be spending

We need to do a lot more work in the export market, go meet people, talk to them, and invite them here. All this has to happen. So, we've already started doing this before the FTA was signed, and there has been some considerable traction with a few companies, and they've shown a lot of interest in what we're doing to see whether this can be deployed, used or modified for their requirements in their avionics and other areas of operation.

We've started getting RFPs from them. Hopefully, this will all turn out to be contracts going ahead. So, we need to -- these are early days. We need to actually do a lot more work in the export market, put a team together, and drive the export market consistently to see that 2 to 3 years down the line, we have a steady business, which can scale.

We have competitive advantages in terms of competencies, which are world-class. Second, our cost of development is far lower than the cost of development in Europe. Third, since everything is done in-house, more than what the large corporations do because they rely on a lot of contracts to do it. We're able to bring economies of scale and cost to them."

Src – Data Patterns Q3FY26 concall, no reco

items.title: RT Rohit Shinde: Why won't wages grow? In India, wages won't grow much until we hit the Lewis Transition. The Lewis Transition point is when a develop...
items.description_html:
RT Rohit Shinde
Why won't wages grow?

In India, wages won't grow much until we hit the Lewis Transition.

The Lewis Transition point is when a developing country exhausts all the supply of rural labor that wants to move out of agriculture and into manufacturing.

Because India has 40% of its labor in agriculture, there's still a lot more labor that needs to be drained out of agriculture and into industry.

There's still a decade to go for wages to rise sustainably.


Swaraj: multiple things can be true at the same time:

- wages have not grown in the last decade or so, especially real wages and at the entry level

- you need wage suppression if you want low cost manufacturing, and our social elites will never allow it to happen since it hurts their

items.title: RT Maadhyam: Devil is in the details!! - A mischief in the #Constitution (One Hundred Thirty First) Amendment Bill, in the name of #womenreservation, ...
items.description_html:
RT Maadhyam
Devil is in the details!! - A mischief in the #Constitution (One Hundred Thirty First) Amendment Bill, in the name of #womenreservation, is to empower #Parliament to decide, from now on, which Census will be used for delimitation and how often delimitation will happen!

At present, Articles 55, 81 and 170 provide for the 'population' to be determined for the purpose of election of President, composition of #LokSabha and composition of State Assemblies respectively.

Presently, this 'population' refers to population on the basis of Census of 1971 (to determine number of seats) and 2001 census (to determine boundaries of those seats). This was frozen in the #Constitution itself, till Census after 2026 is conducted.

Originally, as provided in Articles 82 and 170, number of seats in #LokSabha and State Assemblies were supposed to be readjusted after each Census i.e. once every 10 years. But this was frozen, as is popularly known, due to difference in population growth rates across states.

Now, what is being proposed is as follows:

1. Mandate to use population numbers from Census of 1971 and 2001, till after Census of 2026 has been conducted, will be deleted.

2. It will no longer be explicitly mentioned in the Constitution that every time a Census happens, it will be followed by readjustment of seats i.e. delimitation will be delinked from census and government can choose when to conduct delimitation.

3. Parliament can bring an ordinary law to determine which Census will be used for delimitation purposes as and when it happens.

4. Women reservation will be implemented on the basis of proposed delimitation only, using whatever Census data #Parliament decides to use.

Now, the #Constitution Amendment Bill, in the text of the Bill, doesn't say that the Census of 2011 will be used for the proposed delimitation as that will now be determined by #Parliament later, by bringing another Bill. But the Statement of Objects and Reasons of the Bill says that 'latest published census' will be used, which is the 2011 Census. The Delimitation Bill (an ordinary bill) also refers to 'latest census figures'.

So, for the 2029 #LokSabha elections, the 2011 Census will be used to determine how the 850 seats, including seats reserved for women, Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes will be distributed across states. Who is likely to gain and who will lose?

But whether after Census 2026, which is also supposed to include Caste Census, another delimitation will happen, we don't know. It will no longer be mandatory to conduct delimitation after every Census. Even if delimitation happens, #Parliament can make law to say that 2011 Census will be used.

As for increase in number of seats in Legislative Assemblies, that is to be determined after delimitation is conducted and what the total number will increase to is now known yet.

items.title: RT Monidipa Bose - Dey (মণিদীপা): The Real Bengal beyond Kolkata Marxism and Islamism ~ Watch the dance of human skulls (kapaala nritya) in Gaj...
items.description_html:
RT Monidipa Bose - Dey (মণিদীপা)
The Real Bengal beyond Kolkata Marxism and Islamism ~
Watch the dance of human skulls (kapaala nritya) in Gajan mela from Rudro dev temple in Kandi.

Today is Chaitra Sankranti, the last day of Bengali year, and celebrates Charak pujo/Gajan utsob. It marks the end of month long penance undertaken by devotees for Mahadeva.
￼
The Gajan Mela is an ancient Shiva-centric festival celebrated in West Bengal for the entire month of Chaitra, culminating as Charak pujo or Gajon utsob on the day of Chaitra Sankranti. It is also known as Shiber Gajan, and the devotees express their belief in Bhagwan Shiva through various rigorous penance rituals, such as piercing of tongues and backs, walking on hot coals, etc. 

Video by Dipika Das.

items.title: Crazy Semiconductor Manufacturing Story - Intel once faced an inexplicable drop in yield every night for a few hours, with researchers running in circ...
items.description_html:
Crazy Semiconductor Manufacturing Story -

Intel once faced an inexplicable drop in yield every night for a few hours, with researchers running in circles until they finally realized the cause: cow farts.

Every night between 1 and 2 a.m., the wind would change direction, and methane gas from nearby dairy farms found its way into the cleanroom through the air purifiers

The extra gas from their sleeping neighbors was enough to affect production and drop the % of flawless chips produced in this time window.

Filtering the gas wasn’t possible, so Intel had to pay for three farms to relocate. Since then, everyone scouting a location for a chip factory knows to keep an eye out for cows.

Src -Focus the ASML way book

items.title: RT Shashi Tharoor: The answer can be found in Europe, where the principle of degressive proportionality is applied to the composition of the European ...
items.description_html:
RT Shashi Tharoor
The answer can be found in Europe, where the principle of degressive proportionality is applied to the composition of the European Parliament — since they have the same problem of small and big states coexisting in one Union.

India also needs a compromise between strict democratic representation (one person, one vote) and the necessity of ensuring smaller political entities have a meaningful voice. It essentially means that while larger populations get more seats, the ratio of citizens to representatives increases as the population grows.

In the European Parliament, the allocation must follow these constraints:
*Minimum Threshold: No member state can have fewer than 6 seats.
*Maximum Ceiling: No member state can have more than 96 seats.
*Inverse Ratio: The "efficiency" of a vote must decrease as population increases. For example, a Member of the European Parliament (MEP) from Malta might represent roughly 80,000 citizens, while an MEP from Germany represents roughly 850,000 citizens.

The goal is to prevent the "Big Four" (Germany, France, Italy, and Spain) from holding a permanent absolute majority that could override the collective interests of the smaller nations, thereby maintaining the federalist spirit of the Union.

Applying this to India is what we need to debate, not women’s representation which no one objects to. We need to address the North-South divide that has arisen over delimitation. A strict population-based reallocation (proportional representation) would drastically increase seats for northern Hindi-belt states like Uttar Pradesh and Bihar, while states that successfully implemented population control (like Kerala and Tamil Nadu) would see their relative political influence diminish.

If India were to adopt a degressive model, the Parliament could be structured to balance population with federal equity. Similar to the EU, a "floor" could be set for smaller states (e.g., Goa, Sikkim, or the Northeast) to ensure they aren't reduced to insignificance. Instead of a fixed ratio of, say, 2 million citizens per MP, the ratio could scale. A state with 200 million people might have 2.5 million citizens per MP, while a state with 30 million might have 1 million per MP. This is to ensure no state feels disenfranchised. As @revanth_anumula suggests, another factor could be a state’s contribution to national GDP. It would be dangerous for our federalism if smaller states felt their prosperity & human development were being punished with relative disenfranchisement.

One could argue that the Rajya Sabha already exists for federal representation. However, degressive proportionality in the Lok Sabha would provide a "weighted" democratic mandate that acknowledges population without penalizing states for their developmental successes.

Finding a mathematical formula that satisfies both the high-growth and low-growth states, and both the large and small states, would require a level of bipartisan and interstate cooperation that it is in the interests of the central government to promote. I urge PM @narendramodi to initiate extensive consultations, with all parties and with all states, before rushing into a hasty delimitation process that leaves the core underlying issues unaddressed.


Varghese K George: If nothing changes in the existing constitutional scheme, southern States are set to lose their absolute AND proportional share in Lok Sabha. States with low birth rates will end up in a worse situation under the default constitutional option that will kick in after the 2027

items.title: Organised manufacturing accounts for the majority of economic value added through manufacturing and employed 18.5 million people in over 200,000 facto...
items.description_html:
Organised manufacturing accounts for the majority of economic value added through manufacturing and employed 18.5 million people in over 200,000 factories in 2024.

The manufacture of food products employs the most Indian factory workers, accounting for over two million jobs. The manufacture of textiles and wearing apparel are other key sources of factory jobs, collectively employing over three million people.

The manufacture of food products is widely dispersed throughout the country, with Maharashtra being the leading employer. Tamil Nadu followed by Gujarat are India's textile manufacturing hubs. Tamil Nadu is India's apparel manufacturing hub, with more than one in three apparel factory workers in the state alone.

The manufacture of basic metals is largely confined to the mineral-rich states of Odisha, Chhattisgarh and West Bengal, as well as Maharashtra and Gujarat.

How much economic value does organised manufacturing create across different regions in India? Read Ishan Deodhar and @akwaghmare's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/manufacturing-hubs/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Manufacturing #Industry #Economy #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Ranveer Singh said in the movie Gully Boy, "Apna Time Aayega", Ranveer bhai ka time toh zaroor Aagaya Ranveer Singh, in his entire 15-year movie caree...
items.description_html:
Ranveer Singh said in the movie Gully Boy, "Apna Time Aayega", Ranveer bhai ka time toh zaroor Aagaya

Ranveer Singh, in his entire 15-year movie career, earned approx ₹1,650 crore. That's what his entire career — Ram-Leela, Bajirao, Padmaavat, Gully Boy, Simmba — added up to at the domestic box office.

On the other hand, just the Dhurandhar franchise has earned ₹3000 crore and is still counting. Not even 1 month since Part 2 was released!

2 films. One franchise. More than everything that came before it, combined.

This is what compounding looks like in real time. Those "underperforming" years of 83, Cirkus, Befikre? They weren't failures. They were a man quietly building range, resilience, and the ability to carry a 214-minute spy thriller on his shoulders.

The market doesn't always reward talent on your timeline. Sometimes it rewards patience.

Stay the course. APNA TIME AAYEGA!

items.title: India’s Renewables energy transition is fundamentally a debt market problem. India’s plan to reach 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030 and a...
items.description_html:
India’s Renewables energy transition is fundamentally a debt market problem.

India’s plan to reach 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030 and a 60% non-fossil fuel mix by 2035 will depend heavily on how its debt markets finance the transition, according to a new report by the Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis (IEEFA).

The article stresses that 80% of power sector financing still comes from bank loans (mainly PSBs) which was designed for thermal power. Renewables need a different financing structure, one with longer-tenure, low cost debt and deeper bond markets.

Renewable projects share common characteristics:
- High upfront capital intensity
- Low operating costs
- Long asset lives (20-25 years)
- Stable but regulated cash flows

This creates a large opportunity for specialized renewable financiers.

https://asian-power.com/news/indias-clean-energy-push-hinges-debt-market-structure

- Trump will TACO by end of day today or tomorrow saying no we like Iran now, so that the blockade is not needed, so that his cronies can benefit from the temporary spike in crude => most likely

- Blockade happens and is effective, bond yields in the US run up massively, causing another trump TACO later in the week => next on the likelihood area

- China does some war like posturing, things get even more heated - third and very unlikely, lowest probability.

Probabilities aren't guarantees. So you can never say no to anything, but the net impact of all three things has a commonality - fear of things getting worse before they get better.

Bosons change the world by gathering. So do Ideas.

items.title: What's even more unbelievable in the world of semiconductors is the fine-tuning, maintenance, and cows! Yeah, cows! Walk into any semiconductor manufa...
items.description_html:
What's even more unbelievable in the world of semiconductors is the fine-tuning, maintenance, and cows! Yeah, cows!

Walk into any semiconductor manufacturing cleanroom, and you’ll find the lithography machine is always king. The most expensive tool is also responsible for the most critical part of the production process, so it’s vital that it’s kept running at full power at all times.

The other machines are less of a strain on the wallet and handle the rest of the steps, such as etching, heating, and applying new layers to the wafers.

In between all this, the discs are also constantly measured to check that the layers of the chips are still properly aligned.

Lithography machines will work between 98 and 99% of the time if they receive regular maintenance. Just like on a highway, with planning, you can easily minimize disruption by redirecting tasks and traffic to other production lines. It’s the crashes, the unexpected ‘tool down!’ warnings that deliver the most economic damage. And no siren will sound to tell you. They’re usually disconnected: there’s enough noise in the factory as it is.

If a malfunction is expected to last more than 48 hours, it is better to switch the wafers to a different production line and take the reduced yield on the chin. This leaves the robots waiting patiently in line while the ASML employees scramble to diagnose and revive the patient. ‘It’s like needing to solve a difficult puzzle,’ says engineer Gang san Kim. ‘When you find the solution, the relief is enormous.’ You need nerves of steel to handle these delicate devices.

Chip factories might be fully automated, but you can never fully escape human mistakes: loose cables, a missed bolt, falling tools, a fingerprint, or a scratch on a lens. But the worst thing to happen in a cleanroom is water leakage or damage to the mask.

This is the unique blueprint with the chip structure, and it costs hundreds of thousands of dollars to replace. As soon as something is wrong with the masks, the top bosses in the factory swarm around the machine. ‘Too many captains, not enough crew,’ as Kim says.

The managers watch anxiously while he delves under the hood of the machine to find the problem. They dare not get involved – they know the body of wires and lenses is far too complex for them to interfere with. It can take weeks to untangle the Gordian knot. And if ASML’s legion of nurses can’t work it out, the specialists in Veldhoven are summoned. Time is everything, so the longer a problem persists, the higher up the chain of escalation it goes, all the way to the original designers themselves.

Wim Pas remembers his white whale clearly. The site manager for the TSMC fabs north of Phoenix ran into trouble with a notorious immersion device; it would inexplicably produce faulty chips for two hours after every restart, and then run seamlessly as if nothing had happened. It took months of research to figure out why. The second the machine stopped, five droplets of water would fall on a layer of glue, causing it to expand by just a few nanometers. This was all it needed to throw off the chip pattern. And once the moisture evaporated, the glue would shrink, leaving no trace of any error with the machine.

Problems like this only come to light in the harsh reality of the factory floor. Even with years of meticulous planning, half the issues these machines face could never be imagined. This is exactly why ASML leaves the fine-tuning of the design for when the engines are running. It is also the only way to find which parts wear out too quickly.

As Pas says, ‘It’s only here that everything wrong with the design reveals itself.’ Then you have to hope the spare part is available – with a device comprised of so many different components, it’s almost impossible to predict and prepare in advance for what will be the first to go.

ASML’s sales department takes care of negotiations with the chip factory over which improvements fall under the warranty and which upgrades they have to pay for. The service engineers steer clear of any financial discussions. Their day-to-day work on the floors of the fabs is pressure enough. They’re on the ground, right in the line of fire: they can do without the distractions.

The lithography machine is eventually optimized using special ‘recipes’. Like a Formula 1 car getting a separate tuning for each circuit, the machines are carefully fine-tuned to the type of chips that are being produced. Chip manufacturers want each device to operate at ‘full capacity’, but as Wim Pas explains, not every machine is identical.

‘Imagine you buy two identical cars. But one can reach 150 kilometers per hour, and the other 180. It’s my job to convince the chip manufacturers to be happy about having a faster car and not to moan about the one that stops at 150.’

Just as Coca-Cola won’t give away its secret recipe, formulas for the chip manufacturers' production are kept close to their chests. However, if you tinker too much and create problems with your machine, Veldhoven will be in touch to find out why.

Unforeseeable complications aren’t limited to the mechanics of the machines. Despite their best efforts, the cleanrooms can’t always keep the outside world at bay. Earthquakes and atmospheric pressure fluctuations due to thunderstorms can easily disrupt the lithography process. Or cows.

Intel once faced an inexplicable drop in yield every night for a few hours, with researchers running in circles until they finally realized the cause: cow farts. Every night between 1 and 2 a.m., the wind would change direction and methane gas from nearby dairy farms found its way into the cleanroom through the air purifiers. The extra gas from their sleeping neighbors was enough to affect production and drop the percentage of flawless chips produced in this time window. Filtering the gas wasn’t possible, so Intel had to pay for three farms to relocate. Since then, everyone scouting a location for a chip factory knows to keep an eye out for cows.

Src -Focus the ASML way, book


Sahil: The deeper you go into the semiconductor supply chain, the less believable it becomes.

> TSMC, a company on a small island, produces over 90% of the world’s most advanced chips
> TSMC relies on dutch company ASML for EUV lithography machines
> ASML depends on German Company

items.title: RT Intrinsic Compounding: Why Neo clouds, Electrification and Hardware+GPUs as a theme is here to stay Nearly half of planned U.S. data center capacit...
items.description_html:
RT Intrinsic Compounding
Why Neo clouds, Electrification and Hardware+GPUs as a theme is here to stay

Nearly half of planned U.S. data center capacity for 2026 i.e. approximately 12 gigawatts is facing delays or cancellations due to critical shortages of power infrastructure, such as transformers and switchgear.

A Bloomberg report reveals that 30% to 50% of planned U.S. data centers set for 2026 are at risk due to transformer shortages, power grid limits, and supply chain delays from China. Tech giants have committed over $650 billion this year for AI infrastructure, but only 5 gigawatts of 16 targeted are under construction.

Only about one-third of planned projects are under active construction, driven by supply chain bottlenecks, long lead times (up to 5 years), and grid constraints, rather than a lack of funding.

Key Causes for Data Center Delays:

Electrical Equipment Shortages: A severe, long-term shortage of high-voltage transformers and electrical components, with delivery times stretching from 24–30 months pre-2020 to over 5 years currently, is stalling projects.

Grid Capacity Constraints: Utility providers are struggling to supply the massive, immediate power demands required by new AI data centers, which are often delayed while waiting for substation upgrades.

Local Opposition & Permitting: Community resistance regarding water usage, energy consumption, and environmental impact has led to local opposition, with projects being blocked or delayed in several states.

Supply Chain Constraints: While companies seek components globally, reliance on specialized equipment, some sourced from China, complicates procurement.

Impact on Industry:

Delayed Projects: Out of 140 planned U.S. data centers for 2026, only about one-third are under construction.

Stalled Growth: For the first time since 2020, data center construction fell at the end of 2025 despite high demand from tech giants like Amazon, Microsoft, and Google, notes a report by Bloomberg.
